stem: Find the smallest positive integer \( x \) that satisfies all three congruences \( x \equiv [[r1]] \pmod{3} \), \( x \equiv [[r2]] \pmod{5} \), and \( x \equiv [[r3]] \pmod{[[m3]]} \).
solution: The moduli \( 3, 5, [[m3]] \) are pairwise coprime, so by the Chinese Remainder Theorem the system has a unique solution modulo \( 3\cdot 5\cdot [[m3]] = [[M]] \). The smallest positive representative is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just adds the three remainders, which does not respect the moduli.
This is \( [[M]] - x \), a reflection of the true solution within one period.
This is the next solution \( x + [[M]] \); it satisfies the congruences but is not the smallest positive one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
This is two more than the correct value.
This is three more than the correct value.

stem: Find the smallest positive integer \( x \) satisfying \( x \equiv [[r1]] \pmod{[[m1]]} \) and \( x \equiv [[r2]] \pmod{[[m2]]} \). (Note \( [[m1]] \) and \( [[m2]] \) share a common factor of \( [[g]] \), so check the two congruences are consistent before combining them.)
solution: Since \( \gcd([[m1]],[[m2]])=[[g]] \) and the two remainders agree modulo \( [[g]] \), the congruences are consistent and combine to a unique solution modulo \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m1]],[[m2]]) = [[L]] \). The smallest positive solution is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two moduli directly, ignoring that they are not coprime (the true period is the lcm, not the product).
This just adds the two remainders, which does not respect either modulus.
This is a reflection of the true solution within one period.
This is the next solution one full period later, not the smallest positive one.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
This is off from the correct value by the shared factor \( [[g]] \).

stem: A crate of marbles, when divided evenly into groups of 3, has \( [[r1]] \) left over; divided into groups of 5, it has \( [[r2]] \) left over; and divided into groups of \( [[m3]] \), it has \( [[r3]] \) left over. What is the smallest possible number of marbles in the crate?
solution: The three remainder conditions form a system of congruences modulo the pairwise-coprime numbers \( 3, 5, [[m3]] \). By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, the smallest positive count satisfying all three is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the three remainders together, which has no meaning here.
This just adds the three remainders, which does not respect the moduli.
This is one full period (\( [[M]] \)) too many.
This is a reflection of the true solution within one period.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This is off by the third modulus \( [[m3]] \).

stem: How many integers \( x \) with \( 1 \le x \le [[N]] \) satisfy all three congruences \( x \equiv [[r1]] \pmod{3} \), \( x \equiv [[r2]] \pmod{5} \), and \( x \equiv [[r3]] \pmod{[[m3]]} \)?
solution: By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, solutions are exactly the integers congruent to \( [[x0]] \) modulo \( 3\cdot5\cdot[[m3]] = [[M]] \); the smallest positive one is \( [[x0]] \). The count of such integers up to \( [[N]] \) is \( \left\lfloor \dfrac{[[N]]-[[x0]]}{[[M]]} \right\rfloor + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the offset \( [[x0]] \), as if the smallest solution were 0.
This ignores the offset \( [[x0]] \) but still adds the extra 1.
This rounds up instead of down, potentially overcounting when the period does not divide evenly.
This is the raw distance to \( [[N]] \), not the count of full periods within it.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.

stem: A positive integer \( x \) is such that \( x + [[k1]] \) is divisible by 3, \( x + [[k2]] \) is divisible by 5, and \( x + [[k3]] \) is divisible by \( [[m3]] \). What is the smallest such positive integer \( x \)?
solution: The three divisibility conditions say \( x \equiv -[[k1]] \pmod 3 \), \( x \equiv -[[k2]] \pmod 5 \), and \( x \equiv -[[k3]] \pmod{[[m3]]} \). Since \( 3,5,[[m3]] \) are pairwise coprime, the Chinese Remainder Theorem gives a unique solution modulo \( [[M]] \), and the smallest positive one is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the three offsets together, which has no meaning here.
This just adds the three offsets, which does not respect the moduli.
This is one full period (\( [[M]] \)) too many.
This is a reflection of the true solution within one period.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
This is off by the third modulus \( [[m3]] \).
